verifier_values.criteria: States that the statute of limitations was 180 days
verifier_values.criteria_explanation:
This case is nearly identical to the case of Committee to Relocate Marilyn v. City of Palm Springs (February 23, 2023) 88 Cal.App.5th 607 (Marilyn Case) in the "2024-CEQA_Statute_and-Guidelines_Handbook.pdf."  In the Marilyn case the City of Palm Springs filed a Notice of Exemption for a 3-year road closure to build a statute of Marilyn Monroe. "2024-CEQA_Statute_and-Guidelines_Handbook.pdf" at page 49. The Committee filed a CEQA claim outside of the 35-day statute of limitation period for notices. Id. The Court held that the 35-day statute of limitation period did not apply because the City's Notice of Exemption indicated that the project would involve a "road vacation." Id. Under the City of Palm Springs' Vehicle Code, a road vacation meant the road would be abandoned. Id. The Court held that the Notice of Exemption did not correctly describe the project and therefore the 180-day statute of limitations applied, and Committee's claim was timely filed. Id at 50.

This case has identical facts. The City filed an NOE saying that road was going to be abandoned when in fact the road was going to be temporarily closed for 3-years. Accordingly, the 35 day statute of limitations does not apply and instead, the 180 day statute of limitations applies. The Committee's claim was therefore timely.

verifier_values.criteria: States that the CEQA action was filed within the statute of limitations
